volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Andromache (Section 1 - Section 183)

sections.label: Section 1
sections.original: Ἀσιάτιδος γῆς σχῆμα , Θηβαία πόλι ,
sections.translation: O city of Thebes , [*] glory of Asia , whence on a day I came to Priam ’ s princely home with many a rich and costly thing in my dower , affianced unto Hector to be the mother of his children ,

sections.label: Section 5
sections.original: ζηλωτὸς ἔν γε τῷ πρὶν Ἀνδρομάχη χρόνῳ ,
sections.translation: I Andromache , envied name in days of yore , but now of all women that have been or yet shall be the most unfortunate [*] ; for I have lived to see my husband Hector slain by Achilles , and the babe Astyanax , whom I bore my lord ,

sections.label: Section 10
sections.original: ῥιφθέντα πύργων Ἀστυάνακτ ’ ἀπ ’ ὀρθίων ,
sections.translation: hurled from the towering battlements , when the Hellenes sacked our Trojan home ; and I myself am come to Hellas as a slave , though I was esteemed a daughter of a race most free , given to Neoptolemus that island-prince ,

sections.label: Section 15
sections.original: δοθεῖσα λείας Τρωικῆς ἐξαίρετον .
sections.translation: and set apart for him as his special prize from the spoils of Troy . And here I dwell upon the boundaries of Phthia and Pharsalia ’ s town , where Thetis erst , the goddess of the sea , abode with Peleus apart from the world , avoiding the throng of men ; wherefore the folk of Thessaly

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: Θετίδειον αὐδᾷ θεᾶς χάριν νυμφευμάτων .
sections.translation: call it the sacred place of Thetis , in honour of the goddess ’ s marriage . Here dwells the son of Achilles and suffers Peleus still to rule Pharsalia , not wishing to assume the sceptre while the old man lives . Within these halls have I born a boy

sections.label: Section 25
sections.original: πλαθεῖσ ’ Ἀχιλλέως παιδί , δεσπότῃ γ ’ ἐμῷ .
sections.translation: to the son of Achilles , my master .

sections.label: Section 26
sections.original: καὶ πρὶν μὲν ἐν κακοῖσι κειμένην ὅμως
sections.translation: Now aforetime for all my misery I ever had a hope to lead me on , that , if my child were safe , I might find some help and protection from my woes ; but since my lord hath wedded that Spartan Hermione [*]

sections.label: Section 30
sections.original: τοὐμὸν παρώσας δεσπότης δοῦλον λέχος ,
sections.translation: in scorn of his bondmaid ’ s charms , I am tormented by her most cruelly ; for she saith that I by secret enchantment am making her barren and distasteful to her husband , and that I design to take her place in this house ,

sections.label: Section 35
sections.original: τόνδ ’, ἐκβαλοῦσα λέκτρα τἀκείνης βίᾳ ·
sections.translation: ousting her the rightful mistress by force ; whereas I at first submitted against my will and now have resigned my place ; be almighty Zeus my witness that [*] it was not of my own free will I became her rival !

sections.label: Section 39
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ οὔ σφε πείθω , βούλεται δέ με κτανεῖν ,
sections.translation: But I cannot convince her , and she longs to kill me ,

sections.label: Section 40
sections.original: πατήρ τε θυγατρὶ Μενέλεως συνδρᾷ τάδε .
sections.translation: and her father Menelaus is an accomplice in this . E ’ en now is he within , arrived from Sparta for this very purpose , while I in terror am come to take up a position here in the shrine of Thetis adjoining the house , if haply it may save me from death ;

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: Πηλεύς τε γάρ νιν ἔκγονοί τε Πηλέως
sections.translation: for Peleus and his descendants hold it in honour as a symbol of his marriage with the Nereid . My only son am I secretly conveying to a neighbour ’ s house in fear for his life . For his sire stands not by my side

sections.label: Section 50
sections.original: προσωφελῆσαι , παιδί τ ’ οὐδέν ἐστ ’, ἀπὼν
sections.translation: to lend his aid and cannot avail his child at all , being absent in the land of Delphi , where he is offering recompense to Loxias for the madness he committed , when on a day he went to Pytho and demanded of Phoebus satisfaction [*] for his father ’ s death , [*] if haply his prayer might avert those past sins

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: θεὸν παράσχοιτ ’ εἰς τὸ λοιπὸν εὐμενῆ .
sections.translation: and win for him the god ’ s goodwill hereafter .

sections.label: Section 56
sections.original: δέσποιν ’— ἐγώ τοι τοὔνομ ’ οὐ φεύγω τόδε
sections.translation: Mistress mine , be sure I do not hesitate to call thee by that name , seeing that I thought it thy right in thine own house also , when we dwelt in Troy-land : as I was ever thy friend and thy husband ’ s while yet he was alive ,

sections.label: Section 60
sections.original: καὶ νῦν φέρουσά σοι νέους ἥκω λόγους ,
sections.translation: so now have I come with strange tidings , in terror lest any of our masters learn hereof but still out of pity for thee ; for Menelaus and his daughter are forming dire plots against thee , whereof thou must beware .

sections.label: Section 64
sections.original: ὦ φιλτάτη σύνδουλε — σύνδουλος γὰρ εἶ
sections.translation: Ah ! kind companion of my bondage , for such thou art to her , who , erst thy queen , is now sunk in misery ; what are they doing ? What new schemes are they devising in their eagerness to take away my wretched life ?

sections.label: Section 68
sections.original: τὸν παῖδά σου μέλλουσιν , ὦ δύστηνε σύ ,
sections.translation: Alas ! poor lady , they intend to slay thy son , whom thou hast privily conveyed from out the house .

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: οἴμοι · πέπυσται τὸν ἐμὸν ἔκθετον γόνον ;
sections.translation: Ah me ! Has she [*] heard that my babe was put out of her reach ? Who told her ? Woe is me ! how utterly undone !

sections.label: Section 72
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδ ’, ἐκείνων δ ’ ᾐσθόμην ἐγὼ τάδε ·
sections.translation: I know not , but thus much of their schemes I heard myself ; and Menelaus has left the house to fetch him .

sections.label: Section 74
sections.original: ἀπωλόμην ἄρ ’. ὦ τέκνον , κτενοῦσί σε
sections.translation: Then am I lost ; ah , my child ! those

sections.label: Section 75
sections.original: δισσοὶ λαβόντες γῦπες · ὁ δὲ κεκλημένος
sections.translation: vultures twain will take and slay thee ; while he who is called thy father lingers still in Delphi .

sections.label: Section 77
sections.original: δοκῶ γὰρ οὐκ ἂν ὧδέ σ ’ ἂν πράσσειν κακῶς
sections.translation: True , for had he been here thou wouldst not have fared so hardly , I am sure ; but , as it is , thou art friendless .

sections.label: Section 79
sections.original: οὐδ ’ ἀμφὶ Πηλέως ἦλθεν , ὡς ἥξοι , φάτις ;
sections.translation: Have no tidings come of the possible arrival of Peleus ?

sections.label: Section 80
sections.original: γέρων ἐκεῖνος ὥστε σ ’ ὠφελεῖν παρών .
sections.translation: He is too old to help thee if he came .

sections.label: Section 81
sections.original: καὶ μὴν ἔπεμψ ’ ἐπ ’ αὐτὸν οὐχ ἅπαξ μόνον .
sections.translation: And yet I sent for him more than once .

sections.label: Section 82
sections.original: μῶν οὖν δοκεῖς σου φροντίσαι τιν ’ ἀγγέλων ;
sections.translation: Surely thou dost not suppose that any of thy messengers heed thee ?

sections.label: Section 83
sections.original: πόθεν ; θέλεις οὖν ἄγγελος σύ μοι μολεῖν ;
sections.translation: Why should they ? Wilt thou then go for me ?

sections.label: Section 84
sections.original: τί δῆτα φήσω χρόνιος οὖσ ’ ἐκ δωμάτων ;
sections.translation: How shall I explain my long absence from the house ?

sections.label: Section 85
sections.original: πολλὰς ἂν εὕροις μηχανάς · γυνὴ γὰρ εἶ .
sections.translation: Thou art a woman ; thou canst invent a hundred ways .

sections.label: Section 86
sections.original: κίνδυνος · Ἑρμιόνη γὰρ οὐ σμικρὸν φύλαξ .
sections.translation: There is a risk , for Hermione keeps no careless guard .

sections.label: Section 87
sections.original: ὁρᾷς ; ἀπαυδᾷς ἐν κακοῖς φίλοισι σοῖς .
sections.translation: Dost look to that ? Thou art disowning thy friends in distress .

sections.label: Section 88
sections.original: οὐ δῆτα · μηδὲν τοῦτ ’ ὀνειδίσῃς ἐμοί .
sections.translation: Not so ; never taunt me with that . I will go , for of a truth a

sections.label: Section 90
sections.original: δούλης γυναικός , ἤν τι καὶ πάθω κακόν .
sections.translation: woman and a slave is not of much account , e ’ en if aught befall , me .

sections.label: Section 91
sections.original: χώρει νυν · ἡμεῖς δ ’, οἷσπερ ἐγκείμεσθ ’ ἀεὶ
sections.translation: Go then , while I will tell to heaven the lengthy tale of lamentation , mourning , and weeping , that has ever been my hard lot ; for ’ tis woman ’ s way to delight in present misfortunes

sections.label: Section 95
sections.original: ἀνὰ στόμ ’ ἀεὶ καὶ διὰ γλώσσης ἔχειν .
sections.translation: even to keeping them always on her tongue and lips . But I have many reasons , not merely one for tears ,– my city ’ s fall , my Hector ’ s death , the hardness of the lot to which I am bound , since I fell on slavery ’ s evil days undeservedly .

sections.label: Section 100
sections.original: χρὴ δ ’ οὔποτ ’ εἰπεῖν οὐδέν ’ ὄλβιον βροτῶν ,
sections.translation: ’ Tis never right to call a son of man happy , till thou hast seen his end , to judge from the way he passes it how he will descend to that other world .

sections.label: Section 103
sections.original: Ἰλίῳ αἰπεινᾷ Πάρις οὐ γάμον ἀλλά τιν ’ ἄταν
sections.translation: ’ Twas no bride Paris took with him to the towers of Ilium , but a curse to his bed when he brought Helen to her bower .

sections.label: Section 105
sections.original: ἇς ἕνεκ ’, ὦ Τροία , δορὶ καὶ πυρὶ δηιάλωτον
sections.translation: For her sake , O Troy , did eager warriors , sailing from Hellas in a thousand ships , capture and make thee a prey to fire and sword ; and the son of sea-born Thetis mounted on his chariot dragged my husband Hector round the walls , ah woe is me ! while I was hurried from my chamber to the beach ,

sections.label: Section 110
sections.original: δουλοσύναν στυγερὰν ἀμφιβαλοῦσα κάρᾳ .
sections.translation: with slavery ’ s hateful pall upon me . And many a tear I shed as I left my city , my bridal bower , and my husband in the dust . Woe , woe is me ! why should I prolong my life , to serve Hermione ? Her cruelty it is that drives me hither

sections.label: Section 115
sections.original: πρὸς τόδ ’ ἄγαλμα θεᾶς ἱκέτις περὶ χεῖρε βαλοῦσα
sections.translation: to the image of the goddess to throw my suppliant arms about it , melting to tears as doth a spring that gushes from the rock .

sections.label: Section 1.117
sections.original: ὦ γύναι , ἃ Θέτιδος δάπεδον καὶ ἀνάκτορα θάσσεις
sections.translation: Lady , thus keeping thy weary station without pause upon the floor of Thetis ’ shrine , Phthian though I am , to thee a daughter of Asia I come ,

sections.label: Section 1.120
sections.original: εἴ τί σοι δυναίμαν
sections.translation: to see if I can devise some remedy for these perplexing troubles , which have involved thee and Hermione in fell discord , because to thy sorrow thou

sections.label: Section 1.125
sections.original: ἀμφὶ παῖδ ’ Ἀχιλλέως .
sections.translation: sharest with her the love of Achilles ’ son .

sections.label: Section 1.126
sections.original: γνῶθι τύχαν , λόγισαι τὸ παρὸν κακὸν εἰς ὅπερ ἥκεις .
sections.translation: Recognize thy position , weigh the present evil into the which thou art come . Thou art a Trojan captive ; thy rival is thy mistress , a true-born daughter of Sparta . Leave then

sections.label: Section 1.130
sections.original: δόμον τᾶς ποντίας θεοῦ . τί σοι
sections.translation: this home of sacrifice , the shrine of our sea-goddess . How can it avail thee to waste thy comeliness and disfigure it by weeping by reason of a mistress ’ s harsh usage ? Might will prevail against thee ; why vainly toil in thy feebleness ?

sections.label: Section 2.135
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ ἴθι λεῖπε θεᾶς Νηρηίδος ἀγλαὸν ἕδραν ,
sections.translation: Come , quit the bright sanctuary of the Nereid divine . Recognize that thou art in bondage on a foreign soil , in a strange city , where thou seest none of all thy friends , luckless lady ,

sections.label: Section 2.140
sections.original: παντάλαινα νύμφα .
sections.translation: cast on evil days .

sections.label: Section 2.141
sections.original: οἰκτροτάτα γὰρ ἔμοιγ ’ ἔμολες , γύναι Ἰλιάς , οἴκους
sections.translation: Yea , I did pity thee most truly , Trojan dame , when thou earnest to this house ; but from fear of my mistress I hold my peace , albeit I sympathize with thee ,

sections.label: Section 2.145
sections.original: μὴ παῖς τᾶς Διὸς κόρας
sections.translation: lest she , whom Zeus ’ s daughter bore , discover [*] my good will toward thee .

sections.label: Section 147
sections.original: κόσμον μὲν ἀμφὶ κρατὶ χρυσέας χλιδῆς
sections.translation: With a crown of golden workmanship upon my head and about my body this embroidered robe am I come hither ; no presents these I wear from the palace of Achilles or Peleus ,

sections.label: Section 150
sections.original: δόμων ἀπαρχὰς δεῦρ ’ ἔχουσ ’ ἀφικόμην ,
sections.translation: but gifts my father Menelaus gave me together with a sumptuous dower from Sparta in Laconia , to insure me freedom of speech . Such is my answer to you [*] ;

sections.label: Section 155
sections.original: σὺ δ ’ οὖσα δούλη καὶ δορίκτητος γυνὴ
sections.translation: but as for thee , slave and captive , thou wouldst fain oust me and secure this palace for thyself , and thanks to thy enchantment I am hated by my husband ; thou it is that hast made my womb barren and cheated my hopes ; for Asia ’ s daughters have clever heads for such villainy ;

sections.label: Section 160
sections.original: ψυχὴ γυναικῶν · ὧν ἐπισχήσω σ ’ ἐγώ ,
sections.translation: yet will I check thee therefrom , nor shall this temple of the Nereid avail thee aught , no ! neither its altar or shrine , but thou shalt die . But if or god or man should haply wish to save thee , thou must atone for thy proud thoughts of happier days now past

sections.label: Section 165
sections.original: πτῆξαι ταπεινὴν προσπεσεῖν τ ’ ἐμὸν γόνυ ,
sections.translation: by humbling thyself and crouching prostrate at my knees , by sweeping out my halls , and by learning , as thou sprinklest water from a golden ewer , where thou now art . Here is no Hector , no Priam with his gold , but a city of Hellas .

sections.label: Section 170
sections.original: εἰς τοῦτο δ ’ ἥκεις ἀμαθίας , δύστηνε σύ ,
sections.translation: Yet thou , miserable woman , hast gone so far in wantonness that thou canst lay thee down with the son of the very man that slew thy husband , and bear children to the murderer . Such is all the race of barbarians ; father and daughter , mother and son ,

sections.label: Section 175
sections.original: κόρη τ ’ ἀδελφῷ , διὰ φόνου δ ’ οἱ φίλτατοι
sections.translation: sister and brother mate together ; the nearest and dearest stain their path with each others blood , and no law restrains such horrors . Bring not these crimes amongst us , for here we count it shame that one man should have the control of two wives , and men are content to turn their attention to one lawful love ,

sections.label: Section 180
sections.original: στέργουσιν , ὅστις μὴ κακῶς οἰκεῖν θέλει .
sections.translation: that is , all who care to live an honourable life .

sections.label: Section 181
sections.original: ἐπίφθονόν τοι χρῆμα θηλείας φρενὸς
sections.translation: Women are by nature [*] somewhat jealous , and do ever show the keenest hate to rivals in their love .

sections.label: Section 183
sections.original: φεῦ φεῦ ·
sections.translation: Ah ! well-a-day ! Youth is a bane to mortals ,
